summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software developers should learn to use professional graphics editing tools as a fundamental skill. Drawing from his experience at MIX, he observes that development and design are increasingly interrelated disciplines. While developers shouldn't try to become designers overnight, understanding graphics fundamentals—like image formats and vector graphics—is essential. He recommends picking from tools like GIMP, Paint.NET, or Photoshop, and investing time to move past the learning curve of 'crappy' tools like MS Paint.
key_points: Software development and graphic design are increasingly interrelated, requiring cross-pollination of skills
Developers should understand graphics fundamentals: image formats (JPG vs PNG), vector graphics (Canvas, SVG, XAML)
GIMP is free and powerful but has an excruciatingly difficult interface that becomes a learning exercise in itself
Competence in professional graphics tools enables better collaboration with designers through shared toolsets
The goal isn't becoming a designer, but gaining enough proficiency to work effectively and even copy good designs when needed
money_quotes: I believe every software developer should have basic competence in a common graphics editor.

Although GIMP is both free and powerful, the interface is so excruciatingly difficult to use that by the time you become proficient, you'll be able to handle any graphics editor on the market with ease.
Try to wean yourself off crappy graphics tools like Paint.
Technically, this has nothing to do with writing code. But competence in a real graphics editor means you'll have a much easier time working with designers because you now share a common toolset.
Given enough practice with the tools, you might even be able to copy a good design yourself in a pinch.
key_insight: Learning professional graphics tools isn't about becoming a designer—it's about sharing a common language with designers and expanding your effectiveness as a developer.
